main_ingredients: variable — broth base (chicken, vegetable, or tomato)
may contain pasta, legumes, or meat depending on daily preparation
allergens: variable — may contain gluten, dairy, celery, or other allergens depending on preparation

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of soup of the day, approximately 250 g, as typically served at a Subway or casual Western restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Soup, vegetable, canned, condensed — USDA SR Legacy (NDB 06590) and Soup, chicken broth or bouillon, dry, prepared with water — USDA SR Legacy (NDB 06413); composite estimate for a generic restaurant soup of the day
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a generic 'soup of the day'. Values are estimated as a composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for vegetable soup (NDB 06590, ~72 kcal/100 g as served) and chicken broth-based soup (NDB 06413, ~15 kcal/100 g as served), weighted toward a mid-range restaurant soup. A representative mid-range value of approximately 45 kcal per 100 g is used, consistent with a broth-based vegetable or chicken soup served at a casual Western restaurant. Confidence is medium due to the rotating and variable nature of this menu item.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; vegetable soup and broth-based soup analogous entries
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a generic 'soup of the day'. GI estimated at 38 based on analogous broth-based vegetable soup entries in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which report GI values of 38–48 for vegetable and tomato-based soups. A conservative value of 38 (low GI) is used. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (38 × 5.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5 serving factor) / 100 = approximately 5.2 for a 250 g bowl.

hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving of this soup provides approximately 950 mg sodium, which represents a substantial portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request low-sodium preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 38 and a glycemic load of approximately 5.2 per 250 g serving, a broth-based soup of the day is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided it does not contain high-starch additions such as pasta, rice, or potato in large quantities.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The elevated sodium content (~950 mg per serving) is a concern for cardiovascular disease management; saturated fat is low at approximately 1 g per serving, which is acceptable, but patients should monitor cumulative sodium intake across the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Soup of the Day is a rotating menu item served at casual Western and sandwich-style restaurants in Singapore, including Subway outlets and mixed-cuisine food courts. The dish consists of a freshly prepared or daily-changing soup, typically broth-based, which may feature vegetable, chicken, tomato, or cream-based preparations depending on the kitchen's daily offering. Common ingredients include seasonal vegetables, legumes, pasta, or shredded chicken simmered in a seasoned stock. The texture is typically liquid to semi-thick, and the flavour profile ranges from mild and savoury to mildly spiced. Nutritionally, a standard 250 g bowl provides approximately 112 kcal, 6.3 g protein, 3.8 g fat, and 13.8 g carbohydrate, with a low glycemic index of approximately 38, making it a relatively diabetes-friendly option. However, sodium content is notable at approximately 950 mg per serving, which is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is generally low in saturated fat and provides modest amounts of vitamins A and C from vegetable components. It is commonly consumed as a starter or light meal accompaniment in a Western dining context.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a generic rotating 'soup of the day'. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite entries for broth-based vegetable and chicken soups. GI is estimated from analogous entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The rotating nature of this menu item means actual nutritional content varies significantly by day and restaurant.
notes: Soup of the Day is an inherently variable menu item; nutritional values represent a mid-range estimate for a broth-based vegetable or chicken soup as served at a casual Western restaurant in Singapore. Actual values may differ substantially depending on the specific soup prepared on a given day. Sodium content is estimated at 380 mg/100 g based on USDA SR Legacy data for canned and prepared soups; actual restaurant sodium may be higher. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for broth-based soups). vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not consistently reported across the analogous USDA SR Legacy entries used for this estimate. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not consistently reported in the analogous USDA SR Legacy entries. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database for broth-based soups. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous USDA SR Legacy entries. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the analogous USDA SR Legacy entries. Multilingual names reflect the Singaporean serving context: Chinese (今日例汤 / 今日例湯), Malay (Sup Hari Ini), Tamil (இன்றைய சூப்), Hindi (आज का सूप). GI set to 38 based on analogous broth-based vegetable soup entries from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.
